intro:
Whether you are creating a driveway, pathway, drainage bed, or landscaping feature, calculating the right amount of gravel prevents costly over-ordering or frustrating under-delivery. Our gravel calculator converts your area dimensions and desired depth into cubic yards (for bulk delivery orders) and tons (for weight-based pricing).

Gravel is typically sold by the cubic yard for bulk deliveries (minimum 1 yard) or by the ton from quarries and landscape suppliers. One cubic yard of gravel weighs approximately 1.4–1.7 tons depending on material type — our calculator uses 1.5 tons per cubic yard as a standard average.

Common depths: 2–3 inches for decorative ground cover, 3–4 inches for pathways, 4–6 inches for driveways. A 10-foot wide, 30-foot long driveway at 4-inch depth needs approximately 3.7 cubic yards (about 5.5 tons).
howItWorks: Enter the project length and width in feet and desired depth in inches. The calculator converts depth to feet, multiplies all three dimensions for cubic feet, divides by 27 for cubic yards, then multiplies by 1.5 for approximate tons.
interpretationGuide: Cubic yards is the standard ordering unit for bulk gravel delivery. Tons is used for weight-based pricing from quarries. Add 10–15% to your calculated amount for compaction and uneven base. Most delivery trucks carry 10–15 yards minimum — check with your supplier.

faqs.question: How deep should gravel be for a driveway?
faqs.answer: Residential driveway: 4 inches of gravel over a compacted sub-base (6+ inches of crushed stone base). Light traffic: 3–4 inches of gravel. Heavy truck traffic: 6 inches of gravel over an 8-inch crushed stone base. Total driveway depth for proper drainage: 10–14 inches.

faqs.question: What type of gravel is best for driveways?
faqs.answer: Crushed stone (angular, compacts well): best for driveways (#57 stone is most common). Pea gravel (rounded, smooth): better for drainage beds and walkways, not driveways (shifts underfoot). Decomposed granite: good for patios and pathways. #3 crushed stone: used for base layers.

faqs.question: How many tons of gravel do I need per cubic yard?
faqs.answer: On average, 1 cubic yard of gravel = 1.4–1.7 tons. Crushed stone: ~1.5 tons/yd³. Pea gravel: ~1.4 tons/yd³. Dense-grade aggregate: ~1.7 tons/yd³. Our calculator uses 1.5 tons/yd³ as a standard estimate — verify with your specific supplier.

faqs.question: How do I calculate gravel for a circular area?
faqs.answer: Area = π × radius². For a 10-foot diameter circular bed: 3.14159 × 25 = 78.5 sq ft. Multiply by depth in feet for cubic feet. Divide by 27 for cubic yards. Multiply by 1.5 for approximate tons.

faqs.question: What does a cubic yard of gravel look like?
faqs.answer: One cubic yard is a 3×3×3 foot cube. It fills a standard pickup truck bed level full (about 1 cubic yard capacity). A mid-size landscaping truck carries 3–5 cubic yards, and bulk delivery trucks typically carry 10–14 cubic yards per load.

faqs.question: How much does gravel cost?
faqs.answer: Bulk delivery: $15–$75 per cubic yard or $10–$50 per ton (depending on type and region). Delivery fee: $50–$150 per load. Bagged gravel from home improvement stores: $4–$8 per 0.5 cubic foot bag — significantly more expensive per cubic yard than bulk. Use bulk for any project over 2–3 cubic yards.

faqs.question: How do I prepare the ground before laying gravel?
faqs.answer: Remove vegetation and 4–6 inches of topsoil. Grade for drainage (1/8 inch slope per foot away from structures). Compact the sub-base. Install landscape fabric (weed barrier) before decorative gravel. For driveways, add crushed stone base layer before the top gravel course.

faqs.question: How do I prevent gravel from spreading?
faqs.answer: Install edge restraints (metal edging, plastic landscape borders, timber, or concrete edging) along all borders. This is especially important for driveways and pathways. Edging can be purchased at home improvement stores and driven into the ground with a rubber mallet.

faqs.question: Does gravel need to be replenished over time?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gravel gets displaced by traffic, compacts over time, and washes away with heavy rain. Most gravel driveways need 1–2 inches of new material every 3–5 years. Raking and redistributing existing gravel periodically extends time between replenishments.

faqs.question: What is crush and run vs. pea gravel vs. river rock?
faqs.answer: Crush and run (crusher run): angular crushed stone mixed with stone dust, compacts tightly, excellent for driveways. Pea gravel: smooth, rounded, 3/8 inch diameter, good for drainage, decorative beds, playgrounds. River rock: larger (1–3 inches), smooth, used decoratively, poor for driveways due to rolling underfoot.
